Section Navigation

  • Dataset

Dataset#

Dataset abstraction for handling urban water system data.

This module defines the base ‘Dataset’ class used to handle tabular datasets in the Open Water Lab (OWL) ecosystem.

The Dataset class provides functionalities for:

  • data formatting

  • data exploration

  • data filtering using univariate and multivariate algorithms

Classes#

Dataset

Base dataset abstraction.

class owl.data.dataset.Dataset(data, timedata_column='index', data_type='WWTP', experiment_tag='No tag given', time_unit=None, units=None, *, copy=True)[source]#

Bases: object

Base class for handling urban water system data

The ‘Dataset’ class provides utilities for managing indexed datasets, including validation, filtering, and index manipulation. It serves as a common interface for dataset types used throughout the Open Water Lab (OWL) ecosystem.

Parameters:

  • data (DataFrame | Dict | Sequence) – Tabular data. If ‘timedata_column=’index’’, the DataFrame index is used as time; otherwise ‘timedata_column’ must exist in the input.

  • timedata_column (str) – Name of the column containing time data, or ‘index’ to use the DataFrame index.

  • data_type (str) – Type/category of the dataset.

  • experiment_tag (str) – Tag identifying the experiment (date, code, etc.).

  • time_unit (str | None) – Unit of the provided time values (informational; not enforced).

  • units (Dict[str, str] | None) – Mapping of column name -> unit string.

  • copy (bool) – If True, the input data is defensively copied.

data#

Stored data (copied if requested). The time column is NOT converted to index here.

Type:

pd.DataFrame

timename#

‘index’ or the name of the column used for time.

Type:

str

time#

The raw time vector: the DataFrame index (if ‘timedata_column=’index’’) or the values of the specified column.

Type:

pd.Index | np.ndarray

columns#

Array of column names from ‘self.data’.

Type:

np.ndarray

data_type#

Dataset type/category.

Type:

str

tag#

Experiment tag.

Type:

str

time_unit#

Time unit (informational).

Type:

str | None

meta_valid#

Empty per-timestamp metadata frame aligned to ‘self.data.index’.

Type:

pd.DataFrame

units#

Units by column.

Type:

dict[str, str]

Notes

The ‘Dataset’ class is intended to be subclassed for specific data sources such as sensor datasets or laboratory datasets.

absolute_to_relative(time_data='index', unit='d', *, inplace=True, save_abs=True, decimals=5)[source]#

Convert absolute datetime values to relative numeric time (starting at 0) in the given unit.

Parameters:

  • time_data (str) – Name of the column containing the time data; use ‘index’ to convert the index.

  • unit (Literal['sec', 's', 'min', 'm', 'hr', 'h', 'd', 'day', 'days']) – Target unit for relative time.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify this object and return None. If False, return a new Dataset.

  • save_abs (bool) – If True, preserve the absolute time in a ‘time_abs’ column before conversion. (For index-based time, ‘time_abs’ is added as a column.)

  • decimals (int) – Round the relative values to this many decimals.

Returns:

New Dataset when inplace=False; otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • The first timestamp is considered t=0.

  • If the time source is not datetime-like, the method attempts to convert it using

    pandas.to_datetime (raises on failure).

add_to_meta_valid(column_names)[source]#

Ensure one or more columns exist in ‘self.meta_valid’ with all tags set to ‘original’.

Parameters:

column_names (str | Sequence[str]) – Column name(s) to add to ‘self.meta_valid’. If a column already exists, it is left unchanged and a warning is issued.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • Ensures that ‘self.meta_valid’ is aligned with the current index of ‘self.data’.

  • Useful to mark data as reliable for further processing (e.g., filling, filtering).

calc_daily_profile(column_name, arange=None, *, quantile=0.9, plot=False, plot_method='quantile', clear=False, only_checked=False)[source]#

Compute a typical daily profile (time-of-day statistics) for a series.

Parameters:

  • column_name (str) – Column to analyze.

  • arange (Optional[Tuple[object, object]]) – Index range to include in the aggregation. - If None, uses the full dataset. - If DatetimeIndex: accepts datetime-like or (int,int) day offsets from start.

  • quantile (float) – Upper quantile; lower band uses 1 - quantile.

  • plot (bool) – If True, returns (fig, ax) with mean curve and envelope.

  • plot_method (str) – Which envelope to plot: quantile bands or mean ± std.

  • clear (bool) – If True and a profile for this column already exists, overwrite it.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, use only rows marked ‘original’ in self.meta_valid[column_name].

Returns:

Updates self.daily_profile[column_name] with a DataFrame indexed by time-of-day containing [‘avg’,’std’,’Qupper’,’Qlower’]. If ‘plot=True’, returns the figure and axis.

Return type:

Union[Dict, Tuple[‘matplotlib.figure.Figure’, ‘matplotlib.axes.Axes’]]

calc_moving_average(arange=None, window=10, data_name=None, *, kind='mean', ema_alpha=None, ema_adjust=True, ema_min_periods=None, inplace=False, plot=True)[source]#

Smooth data using a moving average or exponential moving average.

The method applies a smoothing operation to selected columns using either a rolling mean, rolling median, or exponential moving average (EMA).

Parameters:

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the smoothing is applied.

  • window (int) – Window size for rolling mean or median. If ema_alpha is not provided, this value is used as the span for the EMA.

  • data_name (str | Sequence[str] | None) – Columns to smooth. If None, all applicable numeric columns are used.

  • kind (Literal['mean', 'median', 'ema']) – Type of smoothing to apply.

  • ema_alpha (float | None) – Smoothing factor for the EMA in the range (0, 1]. If provided, it is used directly. Otherwise, window is used to derive the EMA span.

  • ema_adjust (bool) – Whether to use the adjusted form of exponential weighting. Passed to pandas.Series.ewm.

  • ema_min_periods (int | None) – Minimum number of observations required to produce a value for EMA. Passed to pandas.Series.ewm.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a plot of the smoothed data, if supported.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • Rolling mean and median use a centered window.

  • The EMA is computed using pandas.Series.ewm with the specified parameters.

  • The method is intended for smoothing noisy data and does not alter the

original dataset unless inplace=True.

calc_ratio(data_1, data_2, arange, *, only_checked=False)[source]#

Calculate the average ratio between two time series (data_1 / data_2), within a given range, with optional exclusion of previously filtered values.

Parameters:

  • data_1 (str) – Column name for numerator.

  • data_2 (str) – Column name for denominator.

  • arange (tuple) – The index range (inclusive slicing) within which the ratio is calculated.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, only values marked as ‘original’ in self.meta_valid are used.

Returns:

(mean, std) – Mean ratio and standard deviation of (data_1 / data_2) within the range.

Return type:

tuple[float, float]

calc_slopes(xdata, ydata, time_unit=None, slope_range=None, *, window=1)[source]#

Calculate point-to-point (or windowed) slopes dy/dx.

Parameters:

  • xdata (str) – Column name for x; use ‘index’ to use DataFrame index. If datetime-like, provide ‘time_unit’ to express dx in that unit.

  • ydata (str) – Column name for y.

  • time_unit (Literal['sec', 'min', 'hr', 'd'] | None) – Required if x is datetime-like. Ignored for numeric x.

  • window (int) – Use y.diff(window) / x.diff(window) for windowed slopes (>=1).

Returns:

Slopes aligned with the original index (first ‘window’ rows will be NaN).

Return type:

Series

columns: ndarray#
compare_ratio(data_1, data_2, arange, *, only_checked=False, verbose=False)[source]#

Compare average ratios (data_1/data_2) over multiple non-overlapping windows and return the most reliable one (min relative std = std/|mean|).

Parameters:

  • data_1 (str) – Column name for numerator.

  • data_2 (str) – Column name for denominator.

  • arange (int) – Window length. If index is DatetimeIndex, interpreted as days. If index is numeric, interpreted as the same unit as the index.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, use only values tagged as ‘original’ in ‘meta_valid’.

  • verbose (bool) – If True, prints the winning window [start, end].

Returns:

(best_mean, best_std) – The average ratio and standard deviation for the best window. Returns (nan, nan) if no valid window produced finite results.

Return type:

Tuple[float, float]

copy()[source]#

Deep copy the Dataset (data & shallow metadata copy).

Return type:

Dataset

correlation_matrix(columns=None, *, method='pearson', treat_filtered_as_missing=True, plot_heatmap=False, figsize=(8, 6), annotate=True, vmin=-1.0, vmax=1.0)[source]#

Compute correlation matrix for selected numeric columns and optionally plot a heatmap.

Parameters:

  • columns (str | Sequence[str] | None) – Which columns to compute correlations for. None = all numeric columns.

  • method (Literal['pearson', 'spearman', 'kendall'])

  • treat_filtered_as_missing (bool) – If True and ‘meta_valid’ exists, rows where meta_valid[col] == ‘filtered’ are treated as missing for that column.

  • plot_heatmap (bool) – If True, show a heatmap of the correlation matrix.

  • figsize (Tuple[float, float])

  • annotate (bool) – If True, overlay correlation values on the heatmap.

  • vmin (float) – Color scale limits for the heatmap.

  • vmax (float) – Color scale limits for the heatmap.

Returns:

Correlation matrix.

Return type:

DataFrame

data: DataFrame#
data_type: str#
drop_index_duplicates(*, keep='first', sort=False, print_number=True)[source]#

Drop rows with duplicate index values (keep the first or last occurrence).

Parameters:

  • keep (Literal['first', 'last']) – Which duplicate to keep for each index value.

  • sort (bool) – If True, sort the DataFrame by index after dropping duplicates.

Returns:

Number of rows dropped.

Return type:

int

Notes

  • This keeps the relative order of retained rows (unless ‘sort=True’).

  • If your index is strings/objects, the notion of “first” depends on current row order.

Convert to a DatetimeIndex/number and/or ‘sort_index()’ for deterministic results.

fill_index(arange, index_type='auto', *, freq=None, step=None, inclusive='left')[source]#

Fill missing index values within [start, end] assuming equidistant sampling. Leaves newly created rows as NaN.

Parameters:

  • arange (Tuple[Timestamp | float | int, Timestamp | float | int]) – Tuple defining the start and end value of the index range. The value may be timestamps, floats or int depending on the index type.

  • index_type (Literal['datetime', 'float', 'int', 'auto']) – Type of index to construct. If ‘auto’ is selected, the method attempts to automatically infer the index type from the dataset.

  • freq (Timedelta | None) – Time interval used to construct a datetime index. This parameter must be provided if ‘index_type’ is set to ‘datetime’ and the frequncy cannot be inferred automatically.

  • step (float | None) – step size used to construct numeric index. This parameter must be provided if ‘index_type’ corresponds to a numeric index and the step size cannot be inferred automatically.

  • inclusive (Literal['left', 'right', 'both', 'neither']) – determines whether the start and/or end values of the range are included in the index

Return type:

None

Notes

This method modifies the dataset index in place.

gap_lengths(*, treat_filtered_as_missing=True, min_gap_len=1)[source]#

Summarize contiguous missing segments (gaps) per column.

Parameters:

  • treat_filtered_as_missing (bool) – Count meta_valid == ‘filtered’ as missing.

  • min_gap_len (int) – Only gaps with length >= min_gap_len are counted.

Return type:

DataFrame

Returns:

  • pd.DataFrame with columns

  • [‘n_gaps’, ‘max_gap’, ‘mean_gap’, ‘total_missing’, ‘coverage_pct’]

  • Where

  • - For DatetimeIndex (gap units are Timedelta)

  • - For other indexes (gap units are number of samples (int))

get_avg(name=None, *, only_checked=True, return_scalar=False)[source]#

Compute column averages (mean) for selected or all columns.

Parameters:

  • name (str | Sequence[str] | None) – Column name(s) to compute averages for. - None (default): compute averages for all numeric columns. - str: single column. - list/tuple: multiple columns.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, values marked as ‘filtered’ in ‘self.meta_valid’ are ignored (set to NaN).

  • return_scalar (bool) – If True and a single column is requested, return the float instead of a 1-element Series.

Returns:

  • ‘pd.Series’ of column means when multiple columns are requested or default (None).

  • ’float’ when a single column is requested with return_scalar=True.

  • None if no valid numeric columns were found.

Return type:

Series | float | None

get_correlation(data_1, data_2, arange, *, zero_intercept=False, only_checked=False, plot=False)[source]#

Linear relationship between two series: data_2 ~ a * data_1 (+ b). Returns slope (a), intercept (b), and R^2. If plot=True, returns (fig, ax).

Parameters:

  • data_1 (str) – Column name for the independent variable (X).

  • data_2 (str) – Column name for the dependent variable (Y).

  • arange (Tuple[object, object]) – Index range (inclusive slicing) over which to compute the correlation.

  • zero_intercept (bool) – If True, fit with intercept fixed at 0.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, use only rows tagged ‘original’ in meta_valid for BOTH columns.

  • plot (bool) – If True, plot scatter with fitted line and 95% prediction interval.

Return type:

Union[Tuple[float, float, float], Tuple[‘matplotlib.figure.Figure’, ‘matplotlib.axes.Axes’]]

get_highs(data_name, bound_value, arange=None, *, method='percentile', plot=False)[source]#

Tag indices where a column’s values exceed a threshold (absolute or percentile).

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Column to evaluate.

  • bound_value (float) –

    • If method=’value’: the absolute threshold.

    • If method=’percentile’: the percentile in [0, 1].

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Slice bounds along the index (e.g., timestamps). Either bound may be None. If None (default), no slicing is applied (use full index).

  • method (Literal['value', 'percentile']) – Threshold interpretation.

  • plot (bool) – If True, plot the series with high points highlighted.

Returns:

Updates/creates ‘self.highs’ with a column ‘highs’ (0/1) aligned to ‘self.data.index’. Stores the actual numeric threshold used in ‘self._last_highs_threshold[data_name]’.

Return type:

None

get_std(name=None, *, only_checked=True, return_scalar=False)[source]#

Compute column standard deviations for selected or all columns.

Parameters:

  • name (str | Sequence[str] | None) – Column name(s) to compute std for. - None (default): compute std for all numeric columns. - str: single column. - list/tuple: multiple columns.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, values marked as ‘filtered’ in ‘self.meta_valid’ are ignored (set to NaN).

  • return_scalar (bool) – If True and a single column is requested, return the float instead of a 1-element Series.

Returns:

  • ‘pd.Series’ of column std when multiple columns are requested or default (None).

  • ’float’ when a single column is requested with return_scalar=True.

  • None if no valid numeric columns were found.

Return type:

Series | float | None

get_summary()[source]#

Extended summary of the dataset.

Return type:

Dict[str, DataFrame]

Returns:

  • dict with keys

  • - ‘numeric’ (describe() + missing/filtered rows)

  • - ‘non_numeric’ (counts & missing for non-numeric columns (if any))

  • - ‘index’ (a 1-row summary about the index (start/end/freq/rows))

hampel_filter(data_name, *, window=11, k=3.0, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter outliers using the Hampel identifier.

The method detects spikes by comparing each value to the rolling median within a local window and flags values that deviate beyond a scaled median absolute deviation (MAD) threshold.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • window (int) – Size of the rolling window used to compute the median and MAD. Typically chosen as an odd number.

  • k (float) – Threshold multiplier applied to the scaled MAD.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering process.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • The rolling median is used as a robust estimate of the local central value.

  • The MAD (median absolute deviation) is scaled by 1.4826 to approximate

the standard deviation for normally distributed data. - A point is flagged as an outlier if: |x - median| > k * 1.4826 * MAD. - The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.

head(n=5)[source]#

Return the first ‘n’ rows of the data.

Parameters:

n (int) – Number of rows to return.

Returns:

First ‘n’ rows of the data.

Return type:

DataFrame

property index: Index#

Return the current DataFrame index.

iqr_filter(data_name, *, k=1.5, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter outliers using Tukey’s interquartile range (IQR) method.

The method identifies values that fall outside the lower and upper fences defined by the interquartile range.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • k (float) – Multiplier for the IQR used to define the lower and upper fences. Larger values (e.g., 3.0) result in more conservative filtering.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering process.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • The interquartile range is defined as IQR = Q3 - Q1.

  • Lower and upper fences are computed as:

Q1 - k * IQR and Q3 + k * IQR. - Values outside these bounds are flagged as outliers. - The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.

isolation_forest_filter(columns, *, contamination=0.01, n_estimators=200, max_samples='auto', random_state=0, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter multivariate outliers using Isolation Forest.

The method applies an Isolation Forest model to detect anomalous data points based on multiple input columns.

Parameters:

  • columns (Sequence[str]) – Names of the columns used for multivariate outlier detection.

  • contamination (float) – Expected fraction of outliers in the data. Used to define the decision threshold.

  • n_estimators (int) – Number of trees in the Isolation Forest ensemble.

  • max_samples (int | float | str) – Number of samples to draw for training each tree.

  • random_state (int | None) – Random seed for reproducibility.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results for the selected columns.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering results.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • Isolation Forest isolates anomalies by randomly partitioning the data;

anomalies are typically isolated in fewer splits. - The method operates on multiple columns simultaneously. - Rows identified as outliers are flagged across the specified columns. - The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.

meta_valid: DataFrame#
missing_stats(*, treat_filtered_as_missing=True)[source]#

Per-column missing stats (counts & percentages). Optionally treat ‘filtered’ as missing.

Parameters:

treat_filtered_as_missing (bool) – Count meta_valid == ‘filtered’ as missing.

Return type:

DataFrame

Returns:

  • pd.DataFrame with columns

  • [‘total’, ‘missing’, ‘missing_pct’, ‘filtered’, ‘filtered_pct’, ‘effective_missing’, ‘effective_missing_pct’]

moving_average_filter(data_name, window, cutoff_frac, arange=None, *, kind='mean', ema_alpha=None, ema_adjust=True, ema_min_periods=None, absolute_cutoff=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter outliers based on deviation from a smoothed signal.

The method compares the original data to a smoothed version (mean, median, or exponential moving average) and flags points that deviate beyond a specified relative or absolute threshold.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • window (int) – Window size used for computing the rolling mean or median. If ema_alpha is not provided, this value is used as the span for EMA.

  • cutoff_frac (float) – Relative deviation threshold. Points are filtered when the relative difference between the data and its smoothed value exceeds this fraction.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • kind (Literal['mean', 'median', 'ema']) – Type of smoothing used as reference for filtering.

  • ema_alpha (float | None) – Smoothing factor for EMA in the range (0, 1]. If provided, it is used directly. Otherwise, window is used to derive the EMA span.

  • ema_adjust (bool) – Whether to use the adjusted form of exponential weighting. Passed to pandas.Series.ewm.

  • ema_min_periods (int | None) – Minimum number of observations required to produce a value for EMA. Passed to pandas.Series.ewm.

  • absolute_cutoff (float | None) – Absolute threshold used when the smoothed value is zero or NaN. Points exceeding this value are filtered.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering process.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • A point is filtered if abs(data - smooth) / smooth >= cutoff_frac

where the smoothed value is non-zero and not NaN. - If the smoothed value is zero or NaN and absolute_cutoff is provided, filtering is based on abs(data) >= absolute_cutoff. - The filtering is applied to the selected column without modifying the original dataset unless inplace=True.

moving_slope_filter(data_name, cutoff, xdata='index', arange=None, *, time_unit=None, window=1, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False, max_iters=20)[source]#

Filter data based on a moving slope threshold.

The method iteratively identifies points where the absolute slope exceeds a specified cutoff value. Detected points are marked as filtered and can optionally be removed or replaced.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • cutoff (float) – Threshold for the absolute slope. Points with a slope greater than this value are flagged as filtered.

  • xdata (str) – Data used as the x-axis for slope computation. Typically the dataset index or another column.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • time_unit (str | None) – Time unit used when xdata is datetime-like (e.g., "s", "min", "h"). Required for meaningful slope computation in time-based data.

  • window (int) – Window size used for slope calculation, computed as y.diff(window) / x.diff(window).

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering process.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

  • max_iters (int) – Maximum number of iterations used to apply the filtering procedure.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • The slope is computed as y.diff(window) / x.diff(window).

  • The filtering is applied iteratively until convergence or until

max_iters is reached. - If final=True, filtered values are replaced with NaN. - The method is intended for detecting abrupt changes or outliers in time-series or ordered data.

pca_filter(columns, *, standardize=True, retain_var=0.95, method='percentile', q=0.995, k=3.0, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter multivariate outliers using PCA reconstruction error.

The method applies principal component analysis (PCA) to the selected columns, reconstructs the data using a reduced number of components, and identifies outliers based on the reconstruction error.

Parameters:

  • columns (Sequence[str]) – Names of the columns used for multivariate analysis.

  • standardize (bool) – If True, standardize features before applying PCA.

  • retain_var (float) – Fraction of total variance to retain when selecting principal components.

  • method (Literal['percentile', 'zscore']) – Method used to determine the threshold on reconstruction error.

  • q (float) – Percentile used as the threshold when method="percentile".

  • k (float) – Z-score threshold applied to reconstruction error when method="zscore".

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results for the selected columns.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the reconstruction error and filtering results.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • PCA reduces the dimensionality of the data while preserving most of the variance.

  • Reconstruction error is computed as the difference between the original

and reconstructed data. - Outliers are identified as points with unusually high reconstruction error. - The method operates on multiple columns simultaneously. - The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.

plot_analysed(data_name, time_range='default', only_checked=False)[source]#

Plot values & their types (original, filtered, filled) for ‘data_name’ over a given range. Works for both Dataset and SensorDataset.

  • If self._plot == ‘valid’ : uses self.meta_valid and self.data

  • If self._plot == ‘filled’ : uses self.meta_filled and self.filled (falls

back to self.data if ‘filled’ is missing)

Parameters:

  • data_name (str)

  • time_range ('default' or [start, end]) – If ‘default’: full span. Otherwise slice using the provided bounds.

  • only_checked (bool) – If True, exclude filtered values.

Return type:

Tuple[‘matplotlib.figure.Figure’, ‘matplotlib.axes.Axes’]

plot_boxplot(columns=None, *, showfliers=True, figsize=(10, 6), treat_filtered_as_missing=True)[source]#

Plot boxplots for selected numeric columns.

Parameters:

  • columns (str | Sequence[str] | None) – Columns to include in the boxplot. If None, all numeric columns in the dataset are used.

  • showfliers (bool) – Whether to display outliers (fliers) in the boxplot.

  • figsize (Tuple[float, float]) – Size of the generated figure in inches.

  • treat_filtered_as_missing (bool) – If True and metadata on filtered values is available, values marked as filtered are treated as missing and excluded from the boxplot.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • Only numeric columns are considered for plotting.

  • Missing values (NaN and optionally filtered values) are excluded from

the boxplot. - The method is intended for exploratory data analysis and does not modify the dataset.

plot_histogram(columns=None, *, bins=30, density=False, log=False, figsize=(10, 6), treat_filtered_as_missing=True)[source]#

Plot histograms for selected numeric columns.

Parameters:

  • columns (str | Sequence[str] | None) – Columns to include in the histogram. If None, all numeric columns in the dataset are used.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used to construct the histogram.

  • density (bool) – If True, normalize the histogram to represent a probability density instead of counts.

  • log (bool) – If True, use a logarithmic scale for the y-axis.

  • figsize (Tuple[float, float]) – Size of the generated figure in inches.

  • treat_filtered_as_missing (bool) – If True and metadata on filtered values is available, values marked as filtered are treated as missing and excluded from the histogram.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • Only numeric columns are considered for plotting.

  • Missing values (NaN and optionally filtered values) are excluded from

the histogram. - The method is intended for exploratory data analysis and does not modify the dataset.

plot_missing_matrix(*, treat_filtered_as_missing=True, sort_columns_by_missing=True, figsize=(10, 5))[source]#

Plot a matrix showing missing values in the dataset.

The visualization represents missing values over time (rows) and across variables (columns), where missing entries are indicated distinctly from present values.

Parameters:

  • treat_filtered_as_missing (bool) – Whether filtered values are treated as missing in the visualization.

  • sort_columns_by_missing (bool) – Whether to sort columns based on the amount of missing data.

  • figsize (tuple[float, float]) – Size of the generated figure in inches.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • Missing values include NaN entries and, optionally, filtered values.

  • The plot is intended for exploratory analysis and does not modify the dataset.

replace(to_replace=None, value=None, *, inplace=False, limit=None, regex=None, method=None)[source]#

Wrapper for pandas.DataFrame.replace with metadata preservation.

Parameters:

  • to_replace (Any) – Same semantics as pandas.DataFrame.replace.

  • value (Any) – Same semantics as pandas.DataFrame.replace.

  • limit (int | None) – Same semantics as pandas.DataFrame.replace.

  • regex (bool | dict[str, Any] | None) – Same semantics as pandas.DataFrame.replace.

  • method (str | None) – Same semantics as pandas.DataFrame.replace.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify ‘self.data’ in place and return None. If False, return a new Dataset with the replaced data and the same metadata (timename, data_type, tag, time_unit, units).

Returns:

New Dataset when ‘inplace=False’, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

rolling_iqr_filter(data_name, *, window=25, q_low=0.25, q_high=0.75, k=1.5, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter outliers using a rolling interquartile range (IQR) method.

The method computes local quantiles within a centered rolling window and identifies values that fall outside the corresponding IQR-based bounds.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • window (int) – Size of the centered rolling window used to compute local quantiles.

  • q_low (float) – Lower quantile used to compute the rolling lower bound.

  • q_high (float) – Upper quantile used to compute the rolling upper bound.

  • k (float) – Multiplier for the IQR used to define the lower and upper bounds. Larger values (e.g., 3.0) result in more conservative filtering.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering process.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • The interquartile range is computed locally as

IQR(w) = Q_high(w) - Q_low(w) within each rolling window. - Lower and upper bounds are defined as: Q_low(w) - k * IQR(w) and Q_high(w) + k * IQR(w). - Values outside these bounds are flagged as outliers. - The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.

set_index(keys, *, key_is_time=False, drop=True, inplace=False, verify_integrity=False, save_prev_index=True)[source]#

Extended wrapper around pandas.DataFrame.set_index.

Parameters:

  • keys (Hashable | Iterable[Hashable]) – Column(s) to set as the new index (passed to pandas).

  • key_is_time (bool) – If True, the new index is considered the time axis from now on. (We do not coerce strings to datetime automatically here.)

  • drop (bool) – Drop the column(s) used as the new index.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify this object in place and return None. If False, return a new Dataset with the updated index.

  • verify_integrity (bool) – Check for duplicates in the new index.

  • save_prev_index (bool) – If True, saves the current index to ‘self.prev_index’ before changing it.

Returns:

  • Returns a new Dataset if ‘inplace=False’.

  • Returns None if ‘inplace=True’.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • If ‘key_is_time=True’, this method will reject string-typed time values.

Convert to datetime first if needed.

set_tag(tag)[source]#

Set the experiment tag for this Dataset object.

Parameters:

tag (str) – New experiment tag.

Return type:

None

set_time_unit(unit)[source]#

Set the time unit metadata for this Dataset object.

Parameters:

unit (str) – New time unit.

Return type:

None

set_units(units)[source]#

Set the units for the variables.

Parameters:

units (DataFrame | dict) – Units information. Can be provided as a DataFrame (copied directly) or as a dict, which will be converted into a DataFrame.

Return type:

None

stl_residual_filter(data_name, *, period, k=3.0, robust=True, seasonal_deg=1, trend_deg=1, low_pass_deg=1, seasonal=7, trend=None, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter outliers using residuals from STL decomposition.

The method decomposes the time series into trend, seasonal, and residual components, and identifies outliers based on a z-score applied to the residuals.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • period (int) – Seasonal period (number of samples per cycle), e.g., 288 for 5-minute data with a daily cycle.

  • k (float) – Threshold for filtering. Residuals with |z| >= k are flagged as outliers.

  • robust (bool) – If True, compute a robust z-score using the median and median absolute deviation (MAD). Otherwise, use the standard mean and standard deviation.

  • seasonal_deg (int) – Degree of the seasonal smoothing polynomial used in STL.

  • trend_deg (int) – Degree of the trend smoothing polynomial used in STL.

  • low_pass_deg (int) – Degree of the low-pass smoothing polynomial used in STL.

  • seasonal (int) – Length of the seasonal smoother (must be odd).

  • trend (int | None) – Length of the trend smoother (must be odd). If None, it is determined automatically.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot showing the decomposition and filtering results.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • The decomposition is performed using STL (Seasonal-Trend decomposition

using Loess). - Residuals are computed as resid = data - trend - seasonal. - Outliers are identified based on the z-score of the residuals. - The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.

tag: str#
tag_doubles(data_name, bound, arange=None, *, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Tag subsequent ‘double’ values (near-constant segments) in a series.

A point is tagged as ‘filtered’ when |x[t] - x[t-1]| < bound (i.e., the increment is smaller than the threshold). Outside the optional ‘arange’, data is left untouched.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Column to analyze.

  • bound (float) – Threshold for absolute first-difference. Values with diff < bound are tagged.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Index slice to apply the tagging within. If None, use the full index. Either bound may be None.

  • clear (bool) – If True, reset meta_valid tags for this column to ‘original’ before tagging.

  • inplace (bool) – Only used when ‘final=True’. If True, overwrite this object’s data; otherwise return a new Dataset with the tagged values set to NaN.

  • log_file (str | None) – If provided, append a log line with the number of tagged points.

  • plot (bool) – If True, calls ‘self.plot_analysed(data_name)’ after tagging (if available).

  • final (bool) – If True, convert tagged points to NaN in the output (inplace or returned). If False, only update ‘meta_valid’ (no data modification).

Returns:

  • If ‘final and inplace’, returns None and modifies ‘self’.

  • If ‘final and not inplace’, returns a new Dataset with NaNs at tagged points.

  • If ‘not final’, returns None (only tags in ‘meta_valid’).

Return type:

Dataset | None

tag_extremes(data_name, arange=None, *, limit=0.0, method='below', clear=False, plot=False)[source]#

Tag values above or below a given limit as ‘filtered’ in ‘self.meta_valid’.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Column to tag.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Index slice within which to apply tagging. If None, use the full index. Either bound may be None.

  • limit (float) – Threshold for tagging (strict inequality).

  • method (Literal['below', 'above']) –

    • ‘below’ -> tag points with value < limit

    • ’above’ -> tag points with value > limit

  • clear (bool) – If True, reset tags for this column to ‘original’ before applying.

  • plot (bool) – If True, call ‘self.plot_analysed(data_name)’ after tagging (if available).

Return type:

None

tag_nan(data_name, arange=None, *, clear=False)[source]#

Tag NaN values in a column as ‘filtered’ in ‘self.meta_valid’.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Column name to apply the function to.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Index range within which NaN values should be tagged. If None (default), the whole column is checked.

  • clear (bool) – If True, reset all tags in meta_valid for this column before applying new ones.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • NaN values -> ‘filtered’

  • Non-NaN values -> ‘original’

  • If the column is missing in ‘meta_valid’, it will be created.

tail(n=5)[source]#

Return the last ‘n’ rows of the data.

Parameters:

n (int) – Number of rows to return.

Returns:

Last ‘n’ rows of the data.

Return type:

DataFrame

time: Index | ndarray#
time_unit: str | None#
timename: str#
to_datetime(time_column='index', *, fmt=None, unit=None, errors='raise', utc=False, dayfirst=False, yearfirst=False, set_index=True, drop=True, index_name='Datetime')[source]#

Convert time values to pandas datetime and (optionally) set as the index.

Parameters:

  • time_column (str) –

    • ‘index’: convert the existing DataFrame index.

    • any other column name: convert that column; if set_index=True, make it the index.

  • fmt (str | None) – Explicit datetime format for string parsing (passed to pandas to_datetime as ‘format’). If None, pandas will try to infer the format.

  • unit (str | None) – Unit for integer/float epoch-like values (e.g., ‘s’,’ms’,’us’,’ns’,’D’).

  • errors (Literal['raise', 'coerce', 'ignore']) – Error handling for invalid parsing.

  • utc (bool) – If True, return timezone-aware UTC timestamps.

  • dayfirst (bool)

  • yearfirst (bool)

  • set_index (bool) – If ‘time_column != ‘index’’ and True, set that column as the new index after conversion.

  • drop (bool) – If ‘set_index=True’, drop the column after moving it to the index.

  • index_name (str) – Name to assign to the datetime index.

Return type:

None

Notes

Modifies self.data in place and updates internal time/metadata.

to_float(columns='all')[source]#

Convert values in the given columns to float dtype.

Parameters:

columns (str | Sequence[str]) –

  • ‘all’: attempt to convert all columns.

  • list/sequence: specific column names to convert.

Return type:

None

Notes

  • Non-convertible values are coerced to NaN.

  • Columns not found in the DataFrame are ignored with a warning.

  • Updates self.data in place.

units: Dict[str, str]#
write(filename, filepath='/home/saba/GitHub_personal/OWL/openwaterlab.github.io', *, method='all', sep='auto', na_rep='', float_format=None, index=True, index_label=None, include_units=False, default_ext='.csv')[source]#

Write a data export to disk. The output format and compression are inferred from the filename. If no file extension is provided, a default extension is appended.

Parameters:

  • filename (str) – Name of the output file. May include compression extensions such as .gz, .bz2, .xz, or .zip.

  • filepath (str | PathLike) – Directory where the file will be written.

  • method (Literal['all', 'filtered', 'filled']) – Specifies which version of the dataset to export.

  • sep (str) – Field delimiter. If "auto", a comma is used for CSV files and a tab character (\t) otherwise.

  • na_rep (str) – String representation for missing values.

  • float_format (str | None) – Format string for floating-point numbers (e.g., "%.3f").

  • index (bool) – Whether to include the index in the output file.

  • index_label (str | None) – Label for the index column in the output file.

  • include_units (bool) – Whether to include units information in the exported file, if available.

  • default_ext (str) – Default file extension to append if none is provided in filename.

Returns:

Path to the written file.

Return type:

Path

Notes

  • Compression is inferred from the filename suffix.

  • The separator is automatically determined when sep="auto".

  • The dataset is written based on the selected method without modifying

the original data.

zscore_filter(data_name, *, k=3.0, robust=False, arange=None, clear=False, inplace=False, log_file=None, plot=False, final=False)[source]#

Filter outliers using a (robust) z-score criterion.

The method identifies and flags values that deviate significantly from the distribution of the data based on a z-score threshold.

Parameters:

  • data_name (str) – Name of the data column to process.

  • k (float) – Threshold for filtering. Points with |z| >= k are flagged as outliers.

  • robust (bool) – If True, compute a robust z-score using the median and median absolute deviation (MAD), scaled by 1.4826. Otherwise, use the standard mean and standard deviation.

  • arange (Sequence[object] | Tuple[object, object] | None) – Range of data over which the filter is applied.

  • clear (bool) – If True, clear previous filtering results before applying the filter.

  • inplace (bool) – If True, modify the dataset in place. Otherwise, return a modified copy.

  • log_file (str | None) – Path to a file where filtering actions are logged.

  • plot (bool) – If True, generate a diagnostic plot of the filtering process.

  • final (bool) – If True, replace filtered values with NaN after processing.

Returns:

Modified dataset if inplace=False, otherwise None.

Return type:

Dataset | None

Notes

  • Standard z-score is computed using mean and standard deviation.

  • Robust z-score uses median and MAD (scaled by 1.4826).

  • The filtering is applied only to the selected column.

  • The dataset is not modified unless inplace=True.
